subsystem: libyang XML tokenizer/parser — the recursive-descent routine lyxml_parse_elem() that parses a single XML element (start tag, attributes, nested child elements, text/CDATA content, and matching end tag) out of an in-memory XML document into the library's lyxml_elem tree. libyang is the C library used by NETCONF/YANG stacks (e.g. Netopeer/sysrepo) to parse configuration and RPC payloads, so this parser routinely runs on network-delivered XML.
provenance: Identified by the libyang-specific vocabulary throughout: the function name lyxml_parse_elem and the struct types struct ly_ctx, struct lyxml_elem, struct lyxml_attr, struct lyxml_ns; the libyang dictionary API lydict_insert/lydict_insert_zc; the logging/error macros LOGVAL, LOGMEM, LY_CHECK_ERR_GOTO, LY_CHECK_ERR_RETURN, LY_TREE_FOR; the XML character classifiers is_xmlnamestartchar/is_xmlnamechar/is_xmlws and the UTF-8 decoder lyxml_getutf8; and the option flags LYXML_PARSE_NOMIXEDCONTENT / LYXML_ELEM_MIXED / LYXML_ATTR_NS. This is src/xml.c from libyang. The sample banner (corpus/train/train_0139_cwe674.c) and header comment 'primevul_c_validation_0224_vuln' are provenance/labels outside the reviewed logic.
trust_boundary: The parameter const char *data is the raw XML document text and is fully attacker-controlled: in a NETCONF/YANG deployment it arrives over the network (RPC/config payload) or from an untrusted file. Every byte the parser walks through the cursor c/e is hostile — element/prefix names, attribute values, text/CDATA content, comment/PI bodies, and crucially the NESTING STRUCTURE of the document. The function has no explicit length argument, so it also trusts that data is a properly NUL-terminated C string bounded at the intended end; a caller passing a non-terminated or truncated buffer turns every *c / strncmp / lyxml_getutf8 read into a potential out-of-bounds access. The int options field is caller-supplied policy, not attacker data.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point declares a recursive-descent element parser. ctx is the libyang context (dictionary + error log), data is the XML text to parse starting at the current element, len is an OUT parameter through which the function reports how many bytes it consumed so the caller can advance, parent is the already-built parent element (NULL at the document root), and options is a bitmask of parse flags. The cursor c is initialized to data and walks forward; e is a lookahead cursor used to delimit tokens; start marks a namespace-prefix boundary; lws remembers a run of leading whitespace for mixed-content handling. prefix/prefix_len hold the namespace prefix extracted from the element name (heap-allocated, initially NULL/0). elem is the element being built, child is a nested element. size is a scratch length filled in by helper functions (lyxml_getutf8, parse_attr, parse_text, parse_ignore, and the recursive self-call). nons_flag and closed_flag are booleans tracking whether xmlns="" cleared the namespace and whether a matching end tag was seen.

The consumed-length output is zeroed up front so early returns report zero progress. The parser then requires the first byte to be '<'; if the current position does not begin an element it returns NULL (parse failure). Note this dereferences *c with no length check — it trusts that data points at readable memory, which is fine only if the caller guarantees at least one valid byte / NUL termination.

After the '<', c advances past it and e is set to the first name byte. lyxml_getutf8 decodes one UTF-8 code point at e, returning the code point in uc and writing the byte-width of that code point into size. is_xmlnamestartchar validates that the first character is a legal XML name-start character; if not, it logs LYE_XML_INVAL and bails. On success e is advanced by size bytes to the next character. Note lyxml_getutf8 is given only ctx and the pointer e — no remaining-length bound — so it must infer how many bytes to read from the lead byte itself.

This loop scans the remainder of the element name one UTF-8 character at a time, continuing while is_xmlnamechar holds. If it hits a colon ':' the name is prefixed (namespace form 'pfx:name'). It rejects a second colon (multiple colons) and, on the first colon, records the prefix length as e - c (bytes from name start to the colon), allocates prefix_len+1 bytes, memcpy's the prefix in, NUL-terminates it, and moves c past the colon to the local-name start. LY_CHECK_ERR_GOTO(prefix, ...) rejects the case where a prefix was somehow already set. Each iteration advances e by the decoded character width and re-decodes. The loop's only stop condition is a non-name character (or the decoder returning something is_xmlnamechar rejects), i.e. it relies on eventually reaching '>', whitespace, or the NUL terminator.

If the name scan ran into the NUL terminator (!*e) the document ended mid-tag: log LYE_EOF, free the prefix, and fail. Otherwise allocate a zeroed lyxml_elem, checking calloc for NULL. The element is self-linked (prev points to itself, a circular sibling-list convention) and, if there is a parent, spliced into the parent's child list. The element name is interned into libyang's string dictionary via lydict_insert(ctx, c, e - c) — c now points at the local name and e at its end, so e - c is the name length. c is advanced to e (end of the start-tag name).

The 'process:' label is the resume point after each attribute is consumed. ign_xmlws skips whitespace. If the next two bytes are '/>' the element is empty (EmptyElemTag): skip them, set content to the interned empty string, and mark closed_flag. If instead the byte is '>' the start tag closes and element content follows: advance past '>' and clear the leading-whitespace tracker before entering the content loop.

The content loop runs until NUL. The first branch handles the end tag '</'. If whitespace was buffered and the element has no children yet, that whitespace is treated as text content (goto store_content). Otherwise it parses the closing element name exactly like the opening name: validate NameStartChar, then scan name characters. For a prefixed closing name it compares the closing prefix bytes against the stored opening prefix with memcmp(prefix, c, e - c) — here e - c is the CLOSING tag's prefix length while prefix was sized to the OPENING tag's prefix_len. It rejects EOF mid-name, then sets size = e - c as the closing local-name length for the subsequent comparison.

The closing local name is copied into a freshly-allocated NUL-terminated buffer str and compared against elem->name both by length (size vs strlen(elem->name)) and content (memcmp). A mismatch means the closing tag does not match the opening tag — logged and rejected. On match str is freed, c advances past the name, trailing whitespace is skipped, and a final '>' is required. If the element had neither mixed content nor stored content, an empty-string content is interned so content is never NULL for a simple element. closed_flag is set and the content loop breaks.

The remaining content branches: '<?' is a processing instruction and '<!--' a comment; both are skipped by parse_ignore which scans up to the given terminator ('?>' or '-->') and reports the skipped byte count in size, after which c advances by size. '<![CDATA[' begins a CDATA section, handled as text content via goto store_content. In each case buffered whitespace is treated as formatting-only and discarded. parse_ignore's returned size is trusted to keep c inside the buffer.

The catch-all '<' branch handles a nested child element. If existing text content is present it converts to mixed content (unless LYXML_PARSE_NOMIXEDCONTENT forbids it): the prior content is moved into a synthetic child node and the MIXED flag is set. Then it RECURSES: lyxml_parse_elem is called on the current cursor to parse the child, passing elem as parent and reusing size as the child's consumed-length out-parameter. On success c advances by size to skip the whole child subtree. This self-call is the mechanism by which nesting depth translates directly into C call-stack depth.

Whitespace bytes are remembered in lws and skipped. Anything else is text content (also the target of the store_content gotos from the CDATA/mixed-content branches). If whitespace preceded the text, the cursor is rewound to include it. parse_text reads up to the next '<', returning a newly allocated string in str and its byte count in size; the content is taken over by the dictionary via lydict_insert_zc (zero-copy: it adopts str). Then, if the element already has children, this text makes it mixed content, handled the same way as the '<' branch. c advances past the consumed text.

This is the third top-level branch from line 80 (start tag neither '/>' nor '>'): the next token is an attribute. parse_attr parses one attribute, returns it and its byte count in size, and c advances. If the attribute is a namespace declaration (LYXML_ATTR_NS) the code binds elem->ns: a default xmlns (no prefix, no attr->name) with a value becomes the default namespace, xmlns="" sets nons_flag to suppress inheritance, and a prefixed xmlns whose attr->name equals the element's prefix (compared via strncmp over prefix_len+1 bytes) is bound as the element namespace. It then loops back to 'process:' to read the next attribute or the tag close.

On successful close the consumed length *len = c - data is reported to the caller. A missing end tag (closed_flag still 0) is rejected. Unresolved attribute namespace prefixes stored temporarily in attr->ns (as a raw string) are resolved via lyxml_get_ns and the temp string freed. If the element has no namespace, was not xmlns-cleared, and has a parent, the namespace is inherited (using the element prefix if any). Finally prefix is freed and the element returned. The error path frees the whole partially-built element with lyxml_free and frees prefix, returning NULL.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Uncontrolled recursion (CWE-674). Each nested child element is parsed by a fresh call to lyxml_parse_elem, so the C call-stack depth grows one frame per level of XML nesting. Nesting depth is entirely attacker-controlled and unbounded: a document of the form '...' (or even just a long run of unclosed opening tags '...') drives the recursion arbitrarily deep BEFORE any close is required. There is no depth counter, no options-based limit, and no tail-call transformation — each frame carries sizeable locals (c, e, start, lws, str, prefix, uc, size, elem, child, attr, two flags). A few tens-of-thousands of levels exhaust the thread stack and the process crashes (SIGSEGV) — a remotely-triggerable denial of service on any NETCONF/YANG endpoint that parses attacker XML. The invariant the implementation must uphold: bound recursion (or convert to an explicit heap-managed work stack / pass and enforce a maximum nesting depth) so document structure cannot be turned directly into stack depth. This matches the sample's CWE-674 focus.

Out-of-bounds read via unbounded UTF-8 decode (CWE-125, latent in lyxml_getutf8). lyxml_getutf8 is called with only ctx and a bare pointer e — no remaining-length bound. A UTF-8 lead byte announces a 2-, 3-, or 4-byte sequence, so if a truncated/crafted multi-byte lead byte sits at the very end of the buffer (or immediately before the NUL), the decoder reads the following continuation bytes past the intended end of data. The same call appears in every name scan (lines 25, 31, 51, 96, 102, 117), so both opening and closing name parsing are exposed. Because the name-scan while-loops (lines 32, 103) only stop on a non-name character, a name that never terminates keeps the decoder walking forward. The invariant: lyxml_getutf8 must be given, and must respect, the number of readable bytes remaining, and must reject an incomplete trailing sequence rather than read continuation bytes speculatively. This is the classic libyang UTF-8 over-read class.

Out-of-bounds read of the prefix buffer (CWE-125). The prefix buffer was allocated to exactly prefix_len+1 bytes from the OPENING tag (line 44). Here, while parsing the CLOSING tag, the comparison length is e - c — the closing tag's prefix length, which the attacker controls independently. If the closing prefix is longer than the opening prefix (e.g. open '<a:x>' with prefix 'a', close '</abcdef:x>'), memcmp reads e - c bytes from prefix and runs past its prefix_len+1 allocation, an out-of-bounds heap read that can crash or leak adjacent heap bytes into the comparison result (and thereby influence the accept/reject decision). The invariant: the comparison must first check that the closing prefix length equals prefix_len before comparing the bytes, so the memcmp length can never exceed the allocation.

Possible NULL pointer dereference (CWE-476, latent). The error guard only fires when BOTH str is NULL AND size is 0. If parse_text returns str == NULL while reporting a non-zero size (e.g. an allocation failure or an internal error path that still advanced), execution falls through to lydict_insert_zc(ctx, str) with a NULL string. lydict_insert_zc is the zero-copy dictionary insert that adopts/strlen's the pointer; passing NULL will dereference it and crash. The invariant: str must be validated non-NULL before it is handed to the dictionary (the guard should be 'if (!str) goto error', not '!str && !size'). Confirm parse_text's contract for the (NULL, non-zero-size) return.

No explicit length bound / reliance on NUL termination (CWE-20 / CWE-125). The signature carries no length for data; the whole parser advances the cursor and stops on '\0' (the '!*e' and 'while (*c)' checks) or on fixed-count reads like strncmp(c, "<![CDATA[", 9) (line 174) and strncmp("/>", c, 2) (line 75). If a caller passes a buffer that is not NUL-terminated at the intended end, or that is shorter than a fixed strncmp count near the boundary, every such read walks past the buffer — an over-read whose only backstop is that the surrounding memory happens to contain a NUL or a mismatch. The invariant: the caller MUST supply a NUL-terminated buffer sized so the terminator is reachable, and ideally the function should take and honor an explicit length. Confirm all call sites guarantee termination.

Cursor advance fully trusts helper-reported sizes (CWE-20, latent). After parse_attr (line 240/244), parse_ignore (lines 159/170), parse_text (line 216/221), and the recursive self-call (line 200/204), the cursor is advanced by the size those helpers report with no cross-check that c stayed within data's bounds. If any helper mis-reports size (e.g. counts a terminator it did not actually find, or returns a length that overshoots on malformed input), c is pushed past the end of the buffer and subsequent *c reads over-read. The invariant: every helper must return a size that keeps c <= data+buffer_len, and this parser should treat those sizes as untrusted and re-validate the cursor against a known end pointer rather than a bare '\0' scan.

summary: This is libyang's recursive XML element parser (lyxml_parse_elem, src/xml.c) operating directly on attacker-controlled document text. The dominant risk is uncontrolled recursion (CWE-674): the self-call at line 200 makes C stack depth equal to XML nesting depth with no limit, giving a trivial remote stack-exhaustion DoS. Secondary, lower-confidence but real contracts: an unbounded UTF-8 decode (lyxml_getutf8 gets no length) enabling an out-of-bounds read, a length-mismatched memcmp of the namespace prefix against its fixed opening-tag allocation, a NULL-deref path when parse_text returns (NULL, nonzero size), and general reliance on NUL termination / helper-reported sizes with no explicit buffer bound.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: lyxml_parse_elem(struct ly_ctx *ctx, const char *data, unsigned int *len, struct lyxml_elem *parent, int options) [line 2]
reviewed_anchors.reason: Recursive-descent entry point; carries the uncontrolled-recursion and no-length-bound contracts.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: const char *data (input buffer, no length param) [line 2]
reviewed_anchors.reason: Attacker-controlled XML with no explicit length; parser relies on NUL termination — over-read contract.
Out-parameter reporting consumed bytes; c >= data always, so the subtraction cannot underflow — no security contract.
Self-recursion per nesting level with no depth bound — CWE-674 stack exhaustion.
UTF-8 decoder given no remaining-length bound; multi-byte lead byte near end reads past buffer — CWE-125.
Loop advances e with no explicit end pointer; termination depends on non-name char or NUL — feeds the getutf8 over-read.
NULL-checked at line 45; prefix_len+1 with an 8-byte-max element prefix cannot realistically overflow the multiply — allocation itself is sound.
Non-negative pointer difference used to size the prefix allocation; safe as written.
Copies exactly prefix_len bytes into a prefix_len+1 buffer just allocated from the same span — in-bounds.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: memcmp(prefix, c, e - c) closing-tag prefix compare [line 109]
reviewed_anchors.reason: Compares e-c (closing prefix len) bytes against a buffer sized to the OPENING prefix_len — out-of-bounds read when closing prefix is longer (CWE-125).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: size = e - c; str = malloc((size + 1)...); memcpy(str, c, e - c) [lines 125-129]
reviewed_anchors.reason: size derived from e-c, buffer sized size+1, copy length e-c == size — internally consistent, NULL-checked; over-read risk lives in the name scan, not here.
Length-guards the memcmp (size checked against strlen first) so the compare cannot exceed either buffer — correct pattern.
prefix holds prefix_len+1 allocated bytes and attr->name is NUL-terminated; strncmp stops at the shorter NUL — in-bounds.
Cursor advanced by helper-reported size with no re-validation against a buffer end — over-run if size overshoots on malformed input.
Same helper-size-trust class as parse_ignore; folded into that finding rather than repeated per call site.
Guard requires str NULL AND size 0; a (NULL, nonzero-size) return falls through to lydict_insert_zc(NULL) — CWE-476.
Zero-copy dictionary insert dereferences str; NULL str from parse_text crashes — part of the CWE-476 finding.
All allocations are NULL-checked via LY_CHECK_ERR_RETURN/LY_CHECK_ERR_GOTO before use — no unchecked-return bug.
Interns name with explicit length e-c (copying insert); bounded by the validated name span — no contract violated here.
Fixed-count reads near the buffer boundary can over-read if data is not NUL-terminated — covered by the no-length-bound finding.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: prefix lifetime: free(prefix) at lines 55,286,291 and reuse across recursion
reviewed_anchors.reason: prefix is a per-call local (each recursive frame has its own); freed on every exit path exactly once — no double-free or UAF.
Boolean/flag bookkeeping for content-model correctness; no memory-safety contract.
Single cleanup of the partially-built element and prefix; elem is either NULL or fully owned by this frame — no double-free.

findings.title: Uncontrolled recursion on XML nesting depth (remote stack exhaustion)
findings.invariant: XML nesting depth must be bounded before it becomes C call-stack depth: enforce a maximum element-nesting limit (e.g. via options/ctx) or parse iteratively with a heap-managed work stack, so an attacker-chosen depth cannot exhaust the thread stack.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted document with very deep nesting ('<a><a><a>...' repeated tens of thousands of times, closed or not) drives one recursive frame per level with no limit. The thread stack is exhausted and the process receives SIGSEGV — a remotely-triggerable denial of service against any NETCONF/YANG service that parses attacker-supplied XML. No memory is corrupted, but availability is lost.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no nesting-depth counter enforced anywhere on the recursion path (in options, ctx, or a wrapper), and measure the crash depth against the default stack size.

findings.title: Unbounded UTF-8 decode enables out-of-bounds read while scanning names
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — lyxml_getutf8 in libyang xml.c; called here at lines 25, 31, 51, 96, 102, 117
findings.invariant: The UTF-8 decoder must be given, and must respect, the number of readable bytes remaining and must reject an incomplete trailing multi-byte sequence instead of speculatively reading continuation bytes past the buffer end.
findings.failure_mode: lyxml_getutf8 receives only a bare pointer e with no length. A crafted multi-byte lead byte (announcing 2-4 bytes) placed at or just before the end of the buffer causes the decoder to read continuation bytes beyond the intended data, an out-of-bounds read that can crash or leak adjacent memory into character classification. The name-scan loops (lines 32, 103) only stop on a non-name character, so a never-terminating name keeps the decoder walking forward.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect lyxml_getutf8: does it bound its reads to a known end, and does it validate that all continuation bytes are present before consuming them?

findings.title: Namespace-prefix memcmp uses attacker-controlled closing length against fixed opening allocation
findings.invariant: Before comparing the closing-tag prefix bytes, verify that the closing prefix length (e - c) equals the stored opening prefix_len; only then memcmp, so the read length can never exceed the prefix buffer's prefix_len+1 allocation.
findings.failure_mode: prefix was malloc'd to prefix_len+1 bytes from the OPENING tag (line 44). During closing-tag parsing, memcmp is called with length e - c — the closing tag's prefix length, which the attacker controls separately. A closing prefix longer than the opening one (open '<a:x>', close '</abcdef:x>') makes memcmp read e-c bytes from prefix, running past its allocation: an out-of-bounds heap read that can crash or let adjacent heap bytes influence the equality result.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no earlier check constrains the closing prefix length to prefix_len before this memcmp, and that prefix is sized only to the opening prefix.

findings.title: NULL text pointer reaches zero-copy dictionary insert
findings.anchor: if (!str && !size) { goto error; } elem->content = lydict_insert_zc(ctx, str);
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0139_cwe674.c (guard); parse_text / lydict_insert_zc in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: str must be proven non-NULL before being handed to lydict_insert_zc, which adopts and strlen's the pointer; the guard should reject any NULL return, not only the (NULL && size==0) case.
findings.failure_mode: The guard fires only when str is NULL AND size is 0. If parse_text can return str == NULL with a non-zero size (allocation failure or an internal error path that still advanced the cursor), control falls through to lydict_insert_zc(ctx, NULL), which dereferences the NULL string and crashes.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check parse_text's contract: can it return (NULL, size>0)? If so this is a reachable NULL deref; the fix is 'if (!str) goto error'.

findings.title: No explicit buffer length; parser relies on NUL termination and fixed-count reads
findings.anchor: lyxml_parse_elem(struct ly_ctx *ctx, const char *data, unsigned int *len, ...) / if (*c != '<')
findings.code: lyxml_parse_elem(struct ly_ctx *ctx, const char *data, unsigned int *len, struct lyxml_elem *parent, int options)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0139_cwe674.c and all call sites (files not provided)
findings.invariant: The caller MUST supply a NUL-terminated buffer whose terminator is reachable within the allocation; the parser advances only on '\0' scans (while(*c), !*e) and on fixed-count reads like strncmp(c, "<![CDATA[", 9), so any non-terminated or short buffer over-reads.
findings.failure_mode: There is no length argument. Fixed-count comparisons near the boundary (strncmp of 2/4/9 bytes at lines 75,86,152,163,174) and single-byte reads read past the end if data is not NUL-terminated at the intended end. The only backstop is the surrounding memory happening to contain a NUL or a mismatching byte.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify every caller guarantees NUL termination at the document end; consider adding and honoring an explicit end pointer / length.

findings.title: Cursor advanced by unvalidated helper-reported sizes
findings.anchor: if (parse_ignore(ctx, c, "?>", &size)) { goto error; } c += size;
findings.bug_class: Improper validation of array index / cursor over-run
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0139_cwe674.c; parse_ignore/parse_attr/parse_text in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: Every helper (parse_ignore, parse_attr, parse_text, the recursive self-call) must return a size that keeps c <= data+buffer_end; this routine should re-validate the cursor against a known end pointer rather than trusting the returned length.
findings.failure_mode: After parse_ignore (lines 159/170), parse_attr (line 240) and parse_text (line 216) the cursor is advanced by the reported size with no bound check. If a helper over-reports size on malformed input (e.g. counts a terminator it did not actually find), c is pushed past the buffer end and the next *c/strncmp over-reads.
findings.what_to_confirm: Audit each helper's size semantics on malformed/truncated input and confirm it can never report a length that moves c past the buffer end.

audit_checklist: Is there ANY enforced limit on element nesting depth before the line-200 self-recursion? Measure crash depth vs default stack size and add a depth cap or iterative parse.
Does lyxml_getutf8 bound its reads to remaining buffer length and reject incomplete trailing multi-byte sequences (over-read at lines 25/31/51/96/102/117)?
At line 109, is the closing-tag prefix length (e - c) constrained to equal the opening prefix_len before memcmp, so it cannot read past the prefix_len+1 allocation?
Can parse_text return (NULL, size>0)? If so, line 220 lydict_insert_zc(ctx, NULL) is a reachable NULL deref — tighten the guard to 'if (!str)'.
Do all callers of lyxml_parse_elem guarantee data is NUL-terminated at the document end, given there is no length parameter?
Are the sizes returned by parse_ignore/parse_attr/parse_text/recursion re-validated against a known buffer end before 'c += size', or is the cursor trusted blindly?
Confirm prefix is a per-frame local with exactly one free on every exit path (lines 55/286/291) and cannot be double-freed across the recursion.
